issue_title: Updates to imported scss files are not applied
issue_body:
# 🐛 bug report Updates to imported scss files are not applied. ## 🎛 Configuration (.babelrc, package.json, cli command) package.json ```js { "name": "demo", "version": "1.0.0", "scripts": { "start": "parcel serve ./src/index.html", "build": "parcel build ./src/index.html" }, "devDependencies": { "parcel": "^2.0.0-beta.1", "sass": "^1.26.10" } } ``` ## 🤔 Expected Behavior When values in imported scss files e.g. `src/theme.scss` are changed, the output css file should contain the change. ## 😯 Current Behavior Parcel rebuilds the output css files, but the change is not included. However if after you make the change you update the root `src/app.scss` file, the change _will_ be in the output, so imports are working, but something is wrong when re-computing leaf dependency changes but not root changes. ## 💁 Possible Solution There is likely an issue in the imported file caching logic. If ` --no-cache` is applied, the change is picked up on page reload but not but by the hot css reloading. ## 💻 Code Sample src/app.scss ```scss @import "theme"; ``` src/theme.scss ```scss // e.g. changes to this color will not be applied unless the root app.scss file is changed. h1 { color: red; } ``` src/index.html ```html

I should change color

``` ## 🌍 Your Environment | Software | Version(s) | | ---------------- | ---------- | | Parcel | 2.0.0-beta.1 | Node | v10.22.0 | npm/Yarn | npm 6.14.6 | Operating System | MacOS 10.15.6
pr_title: Invalidate cache for environment variable and included file changes
pr_body:
Depends on #4963. Fixes #3708. Fixes #4264. Fixes #5132.

This invalidates the transformation cache when environment variables that are used in the code are updated, or when included files change. It adds an `env` node type to the request graph to track environment variables, and invalidates requests connected to changed vars on startup. Included files now store a hash as part of the `File` node, which is computed while the asset is being committed. Both of these are also added the the asset's `outputHash`, which is used to compute the hash of the asset graph and also in the packager cache key.

Actual invalidation of the transformer cache based on these is a bit tricky, and I'm not entirely sure I have the right solution here. Because we don't (and can't) know what the used environment variables and included files will be until after transforming the file, they can't be part of the cache key. Therefore, since they are already stored as part of the asset itself, we read the cache entry, and then check the referenced file hashes and environment variables against the current values. If they do not match, we don't use the cache entry and instead re-run the transformation pipeline. This works well for local caches, but could potentially cause multiple instances with different vars/included files overwriting each other in a shared cache.

An alternative would be to somehow send the environment variables and included files from the request graph to the transformer when available, and have it use these values as part of the cache key. But unfortunately, this would invalidate more than needed potentially, because intermediary assets that are created during transformation are not stored in the graph, and are only available in the cache itself. For example, for an HTML file with inline CSS, and JS that uses an environment variable, only the JS depends on the environment variable, not the HTML or CSS, so if the environment variable was part of the cache key for all of these assets, it would invalidate more than needed. Same for simpler cases, e.g. TS -> JS. The TS compilation doesn't need to be re-computed, just the JS pipeline. 🤔
